Bloomberg.heading: JPMorgan Analysts See AI Disruption Spurring Small Bank Mergers
Bloomberg.summary: Analysts at JPMorgan Chase, led by Vivek Juneja, suggest that smaller US banks struggling to match larger banks' artificial intelligence (AI) investments may face pressure to merge to offset potential revenue impacts. Most banks are investing in AI to stay competitive, but concerns over AI’s effect on earnings have contributed to a recent sector selloff. Larger banks and some regional lenders, with greater AI investment capacity, are expected to be better positioned, while smaller banks may have to consolidate due to limited resources. Last week, US bank stocks fell 5.5%, underperforming the 1.4% decline in the S&P 500, amid investor worries about AI’s impact on corporate profits. Larger banks have lagged regional peers this year, as they generate more revenue from areas like wealth management and investment banking, sectors considered vulnerable to AI disruption. Nonetheless, Juneja notes money center banks could offset AI challenges through trading revenue gains and their stronger AI investment capabilities. He does not foresee mid-sized or larger regional banks selling but acknowledges the risk of underinvestment at some institutions.

Bloomberg.heading: Apple Ramps Up Work on Glasses, Pendant, and Camera AirPods for AI Era
Bloomberg.summary:
Apple Inc. is accelerating development of three new AI-powered wearable devices: smart glasses, a pendant accessory, and enhanced AirPods. These products, integrated with the Siri digital assistant, will leverage camera systems to provide visual context for user interactions and connect with the iPhone. The smart glasses, code-named N50, are the most advanced, featuring high-resolution and computer vision cameras to interpret surroundings and assist with tasks like object identification, text recognition, and context-aware reminders. Production is targeted to begin as early as December, with a planned release in 2027. The glasses will lack a display, relying instead on audio and visual inputs, and be built with premium materials in various styles developed in-house.

The pendant, designed to be worn clipped to clothing or as a necklace, serves as an always-on camera and microphone for Siri input, functioning as an extension of the phone. It is a low-power device dependent on the iPhone for processing and may launch as early as next year, though the project is still preliminary.

The upgraded AirPods will incorporate camera technology and expanded AI features, complementing existing capabilities such as live translation. These are expected to be simpler and more affordable than the glasses.

Apple is focusing on AI integration to enhance user experience amid challenges upgrading Siri, which is set for a chatbot-like revamp in iOS 27 later this year. The company aims to compete with similar offerings from Meta and OpenAI and hopes to strengthen its ecosystem through these devices. Apple has also discontinued some past projects, like wrist-worn cameras and a lower-cost Vision Pro headset. Beyond wearables, Apple is developing AI-driven home products including smart displays, HomePods, and indoor sensors.

Bloomberg.heading: BofA Survey Shows Investor Worry Over Capex Race at Record High
Bloomberg.summary:
Bank of America’s latest fund manager survey shows a record 35% of investors believe companies are overinvesting, the highest level in two decades. Despite the caution on spending, investor optimism is at its highest since June 2021. The survey highlights expectations of record capital expenditure in 2026, with the four largest US tech firms projecting a combined $650 billion in spending. Market reactions vary, with Microsoft shares dropping sharply due to concerns over investment payback periods, while Meta Platforms gained over 11% after announcing substantial AI investment plans.

About 25% of respondents identified an “AI bubble” as the top market risk, and 30% viewed AI-related capital spending by major tech companies as a potential trigger for a credit crisis. Although investors regard current capital spending as excessive, they also hold the most positive outlook on global corporate earnings since August 2021.

The survey revealed a shift away from technology stocks and the US dollar, with increased allocations in energy, materials, and consumer staples. The reduction in tech exposure is the largest since March 2025, dropping from a net 19% overweight to just net 5%. Additionally, investors are favoring emerging and European markets over US stocks, marking the highest level of such rotation since February 2021.

The survey also indicates a preference for small-cap stocks over large caps, reflecting the ongoing rotation away from tech giants. Conducted between February 6-12, the survey included 162 participants managing $440 billion in assets.

Bloomberg.heading: Goldman Plans to Remove DEI Criteria for Board Members, WSJ Says
Bloomberg.summary:
Goldman Sachs Group Inc. plans to remove diversity criteria, including race, gender identity, and sexual orientation, from its board candidate selection factors. This potential change follows a request from the National Legal and Policy Center, a nonprofit opposing diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) rules. Goldman Sachs is expected to approve these changes soon, affecting its board nominations process. Currently, the bank’s board includes five women and nine men; among the 14 members, one identifies as multiracial and one as Asian.

This move aligns with similar rollbacks at other Wall Street banks such as Citigroup, Bank of America, and Wells Fargo, which have reduced or eliminated certain DEI policies. Citigroup, for example, has ended diversity hiring goals and interview requirements for diverse candidates. These actions coincide with federal policy trends initiated under the Trump administration, which issued an executive order directing federal contractors to discontinue DEI programs considered discriminatory. A recent federal appellate court upheld such directives.

Goldman Sachs has also already removed diversity targets from regulatory filings and abandoned a previous commitment to reject initial public offering business from all-White, male boards. Support for DEI-related shareholder proposals has decreased significantly, dropping to an 8% average in 2025 from 14% in 2024 and a peak of 36% in 2021, according to a Bloomberg Intelligence report.

Bloomberg.heading: Google Secures Ormat Geothermal Power for Nevada Data Centers
Bloomberg.summary: Alphabet Inc.'s Google has entered into a power purchase agreement with Ormat Technologies to supply up to 150 megawatts of geothermal energy for its data center operations in Nevada. The energy will be provided through Google’s existing contract with NV Energy, a utility owned by Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Ormat plans to generate this power from multiple geothermal projects currently under development, expected to begin commercial operation between 2028 and 2030. The agreement will last for 15 years following the commissioning of the final project. Ormat’s stock rose by up to 8.1%, marking its largest intraday gain in 2023. Analysts from Oppenheimer & Co. noted the deal could highlight Ormat as a key player in AI infrastructure energy supply. Geothermal power is valued by hyperscale companies for its consistent, 24/7 output, which supports emissions reduction targets and mitigates challenges related to renewable energy reliability and storage. Google’s head of energy market innovation, Briana Kobor, stated that the agreement is designed to cover all electricity service costs without burdening other ratepayers and to improve local grid reliability. The deal is pending approval from Nevada’s public utilities commission.

Bloomberg.heading: Home Depot Gets Stricter on Bonuses Amid Housing Market Freeze
Bloomberg.summary:
Home Depot Inc. is tightening bonus payout requirements for managers amid a business slowdown. The company will raise the minimum sales performance threshold for bonuses from 90% to 95%. Managers meeting the minimum will receive 25% of the target bonus, down from 50%. These changes will apply to bonus payments made in September and reflect a return to pre-pandemic incentive standards. Home Depot also maintains a separate profit-sharing program for all employees. A company spokeswoman emphasized the commitment to reward good performance across all staff levels.

The move aligns with a broader trend of employers regaining leverage after pandemic-era labor shortages, as hiring pressure eases. Job growth forecasts for 2025 are among the lowest since 2003, while recent job cuts reached a peak for the month of January not seen since 2009.

Previously, Home Depot adjusted bonus standards to reflect pandemic-related financial variability. The current changes aim to emphasize strong performance and accountability. The retailer faces multiple challenges, including a frozen housing market causing slower business and affordability concerns. Additionally, federal raids at some store parking lots, though reportedly without material impact on operations, have disrupted store environments.

Last month, Home Depot implemented job cuts and announced a mandatory five-day in-office workweek for corporate employees starting in April. The company plans to release its quarterly earnings later this month.

Bloomberg.heading: Teslas Austin Robotaxis Report 14 Crashes in First Eight Months
Bloomberg.summary:
Tesla Inc.’s robotaxi service in Austin has been involved in 14 crash incidents over approximately eight months, according to reports filed with the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA). Tesla began its limited robotaxi operations in Austin in June with around a dozen cars accompanied by human safety monitors and has gradually expanded the fleet. The first reported crash occurred in July, initially described as property damage only, but later updated to include minor injuries and hospitalization. Another July incident also caused minor injuries.

Tesla’s January report added five new incidents from December and January, including a collision between a stopped robotaxi and an Austin city bus, and two incidents where robotaxis backed into objects in parking lots. In January, Tesla started offering some rides without safety monitors after testing the feature in December, though it is unclear if any incidents involved these driverless vehicles.

Unlike competitors, Tesla redacts narrative details in its incident reports, with most citing property damage only. Austin remains Tesla’s sole city offering robotaxi rides, while its San Francisco Bay Area service uses human drivers through the same app. CEO Elon Musk has emphasized autonomy and robotics as key to Tesla’s future amidst challenges in its electric vehicle business.

Tesla has not disclosed the size of its Austin robotaxi fleet, but Musk noted in January that there were 500 rideshare vehicles combined in Austin and the Bay Area. Competitor Alphabet Inc.’s Waymo operates about 2,500 autonomous vehicles across multiple U.S. cities, including roughly 200 in Austin, reporting hundreds of incidents since June, with about 50 in Austin alone.

Tesla aims to expand robotaxi operations to approximately seven more cities by mid-year but missed similar expansion targets last year. The company’s autonomous service remains behind Waymo, which launched driverless public rides in Phoenix in 2020.

WSJ.heading: Can Nvidia’s stock approach $400? Here’s why earnings may mark the start of a big rally.
WSJ.summary: Cantor Fitzgerald analyst C.J. Muse advised Nvidia investors to remain calm amid a period of limited stock momentum. Muse highlighted that Nvidia is entering the early phase of what he terms a "rack-scale super cycle," driven by the expansion of its Blackwell artificial-intelligence chips and associated rack systems. He also mentioned the anticipated release of the Rubin family of products expected later this year as a potential catalyst for renewed stock activity. The analyst’s note suggests that these developments could provide new growth opportunities for Nvidia in the AI hardware sector.

WSJ.heading: Infosys Stock Is Rising. It Just Unveiled This AI Partnership With Anthropic.
WSJ.summary: Infosys shares rose following the announcement of a collaboration with Anthropic to develop custom artificial-intelligence agents. The partnership aims to leverage Anthropic's AI expertise to enhance Infosys’s service offerings. This move is part of Infosys’s strategy to integrate advanced AI solutions and address market concerns about its future growth prospects amid the increasing importance of AI technologies in the IT services sector.

WSJ.heading: Nvidia Stock Falls. There Are Reasons to Believe It Will Rally.
WSJ.summary: Nvidia's stock declined slightly early Tuesday. However, a positive assessment from a Wall Street analyst and strong efficiency metrics for its artificial-intelligence chips may provide reassurance to investors.

WSJ.heading: Resilient U.S. Oil Production Is a Boon to Trump. How Long Will It Last?
WSJ.summary:
U.S. oil production has continued to rise despite predictions of decline linked to low crude prices, driven by technological advancements and industry consolidation. In the Permian Basin, operators use remote monitoring and enhanced drilling techniques to extract more oil efficiently. In 2023, U.S. wells produced a record 13.6 million barrels per day, surpassing Energy Information Administration forecasts. Major oil companies with strong balance sheets have replaced smaller, debt-reliant frackers, allowing steady output despite price fluctuations. Innovations include longer horizontal wells and improved fracking methods that boost production while reducing costs. Chevron, for example, has significantly increased output with fewer rigs and frack crews than in 2019.

However, some analysts warn U.S. production will plateau and eventually decline as prime drilling locations are depleted. Production is expected to stabilize this year before decreasing by 2027, with diminished "sweet spots" requiring more complex operations. The industry is exploring new techniques to enhance recovery, such as advanced chemicals and using petroleum coke in fracking. The Trump administration acknowledges shale's maturation and highlights the need for technological progress to sustain output.

Smaller producers are scaling back, and some, like Continental Resources, have halted drilling in certain regions. This shift could lead to higher oil prices over time due to reduced capacity to quickly expand supply when demand rises. Overall, U.S. oil output resilience has surprised observers amid global market challenges, but future growth faces significant constraints.

WSJ.heading: Spain to Probe X, Meta, TikTok Over AI Images
WSJ.summary:
The Spanish government has requested prosecutors to investigate social media platforms X, Meta, and TikTok for potential crimes involving AI-generated images of minors, including nude depictions, which have rapidly proliferated online. A government minister reported that about 3 million such images appeared within two weeks, prompting concerns over offenses such as child pornography and degrading treatment of minors. Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez stated that these platforms undermine children's mental health, dignity, and rights.

Meta declined to comment on the investigation but affirmed its strict policies against nonconsensual intimate imagery and child sexual exploitation, noting its AI tools are designed not to generate nude images. X did not respond to inquiries but previously stated its measures to limit image generation via its Grok AI and its zero-tolerance policy toward child sexual exploitation and nonconsensual nudity. TikTok also did not comment but states on its website that it prohibits content related to sexual abuse or exploitation of minors.

The rise of AI technologies enables users to produce images from text prompts, sometimes resulting in explicit content when safeguards are insufficient. Elon Musk’s X platform recently faced scrutiny in Europe following backlash over sexualized deepfake images produced by Grok AI, which is under investigation by the EU for possible violations of the Digital Services Act. Ireland's data protection authority has also launched an inquiry into X over potential EU regulation breaches.

These developments follow Prime Minister Sanchez’s announcement to implement age-verification systems to restrict social media access for users under 16, aligning Spain with similar policies in Australia.

WSJ.heading: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
WSJ.summary:
Barclays analysts project the physical AI market—encompassing humanoid robots, autonomous vehicles, industrial automation, and drones—could reach $0.5 trillion to $1.4 trillion by 2035, with autonomous vehicles potentially accounting for up to $550 billion of the growth. China currently leads in robot deployment and industrial robot adoption, expected to account for over 85% of about 15,000 new humanoid robot installations in 2025.

Wedbush analysts report that Apple is poised to overcome AI challenges experienced over the past 18 months, with the company entering the AI space this year. Apple's partnership with Google Gemini and the addition of new AI leadership underpin this progress. Analysts view recent stock sell-offs as unwarranted and estimate AI monetization could add $75 to $100 per share in the coming years.

Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) has reopened acquisition negotiations with Paramount Skydance but still favors Netflix’s offer, citing remaining unfavorable terms in Paramount’s proposal despite adjustments. Warner has requested Paramount submit its best and final proposal, expecting a bid higher than $31 per share while Netflix has provided a seven-day waiver to facilitate discussions.

According to Bank of America’s February global fund manager survey, 50% of fund managers identify "long gold" as the most crowded trade for the second month in a row, slightly down from 51% in January. Twenty percent cite large U.S. technology stocks, including Nvidia, Alphabet, Apple, Amazon, Microsoft, Meta, and Tesla, as the most crowded trade.

The same survey indicates fund managers are overweight in European healthcare and banking sectors this month. Healthcare has overtaken technology as the most favored sector, though technology maintains a net bullish stance despite reduced exposure amid software sell-offs. Technology is considered the most overvalued sector in Europe, with autos being the most neglected, as nearly 60% of managers reduce allocations relative to benchmarks.

WSJ.heading: This auto-parts maker is jumping on the breakup bandwagon. Here’s why the stock is tanking.
WSJ.summary: Shares of Genuine Parts (GPC) declined sharply on Tuesday following a significant miss on quarterly profit expectations and a disappointing financial outlook for 2026. The underperformance was partly attributed to weakness in its domestic NAPA automotive parts business. Despite the negative earnings report, the company announced a strategic decision to separate into two publicly traded entities after an extensive review. One entity will focus on automotive parts, including the NAPA brand, while the other will concentrate on industrial parts, which includes the Motion brand. The stock decline occurred despite what is typically viewed as a positive corporate restructuring move by investors.

WSJ.heading: Warner Bros. and Paramount Restart Merger Talks. Why Netflix Agreed.
WSJ.summary: Warner Bros. Discovery has resumed negotiations with Paramount Skydance, providing the CBS parent company with a seven-day deadline to submit its best and final offer. This step indicates ongoing discussions between the two media companies, with Warner Bros. Discovery seeking to finalize terms promptly. The development follows an earlier phase of talks, aiming to reach a conclusive agreement within the specified timeframe.

WSJ.heading: Warner Reopens Talks With Paramount After Sweetened Offer
WSJ.summary:
Warner Bros. Discovery announced it will resume merger talks with Paramount after Paramount increased its all-cash hostile bid from $30 to potentially above $31 per share, aiming to acquire Warner in a deal valued around $78 billion. Paramount’s offer includes paying Warner’s $2.8 billion termination fee owed to Netflix if that deal falls through and adding a "ticking fee" of 25 cents per share quarterly starting January 2027. Warner has agreed to a negotiation period with Paramount ending on February 23, with Paramount expected to raise its bid further.

Meanwhile, Warner still favors Netflix’s $72 billion agreement to acquire its movie and TV studios and HBO Max, an all-cash deal reached in December and recommended by Warner’s board. Netflix has a right to match any higher bid and granted Warner a seven-day waiver to engage with Paramount. Netflix called Paramount’s bid process a distraction and affirmed its offer’s superiority.

Warner’s leadership and analysts remain skeptical about Paramount’s ability to close the deal due to high debt and operational covenants that could restrict Warner pre-closing, unlike Netflix’s offer, which Warner says has fewer conditions. Warner plans a shareholder vote on the Netflix deal on March 20.

The takeover battle includes regulatory scrutiny from the U.S. Justice Department, investigating Netflix’s proposal for anticompetitive concerns; Netflix denies being informed of any monopolization probe. Investor Ancora Holdings recently acquired a stake in Warner to encourage negotiations with Paramount. Paramount has criticized Warner for favoring Netflix and limiting engagement during the bidding process.

Reuters.heading: Adage Capital trims stakes in AI heavyweights
Reuters.summary: Adage Capital Partners reported in its latest SEC filing that it reduced its stakes in several major AI-related companies during the fourth quarter of 2025. The firm trimmed holdings in Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, and Nvidia, while increasing its Oracle shares by about 19% to 1.87 million, valued at approximately $365 million. Softbank Group announced the full divestment of its Nvidia stake, a sale it initially disclosed in November, which was completed in October for $5.8 billion to finance additional investments in OpenAI, the creator of ChatGPT. Nvidia’s stock has declined roughly 7% since Softbank’s sale announcement. The company's upcoming quarterly earnings report, due February 25, is anticipated by analysts to show a 67% revenue increase. The filings for Softbank and Adage compare holdings as of December 31 with those from September 30, reflecting positions at quarter-end and not subsequent changes in the new year. Market concerns have grown over whether AI-related investments will yield returns to justify current high valuations after prior multi-year gains.

Reuters.heading: Berkshire-owned PacifiCorp, citing liquidity, sells Washington assets to Portland General Electric for $1.9 billion
Reuters.summary:
PacifiCorp, a utility owned by Berkshire Hathaway, is selling wind, natural gas generation, and distribution assets in central and southern Washington state to Portland General Electric (PGE) for $1.9 billion. The assets include the Chehalis natural gas plant, Goodnoe Hills wind facility, Marengo I and II wind facilities, and 4,500 miles of transmission and distribution lines, serving approximately 140,000 customers over 2,700 square miles. Manulife Investment Management will acquire a 49% stake in the Washington utility business. The transaction is expected to take at least one year, pending federal and state regulatory approvals.

PacifiCorp cited liquidity concerns related to ongoing wildfire litigation in Oregon, where it faces claims totaling up to $52 billion from four wildfires in 2020 allegedly caused by its failure to shut off power lines during a windstorm. Trials could extend into 2028. The company has sought to dismiss emotional distress claims related to the fires in Oregon courts.

PacifiCorp also noted that differing policies across the six western states it serves have pressured its financial stability and credit ratings. The sale excludes PacifiCorp’s hydroelectric generation facilities in Washington.

The transaction expands PGE’s energy portfolio amid rising electricity demand from industrial users and data centers. PGE’s CEO described the assets as a mix of natural gas and wind resources that provide safe, reliable, and affordable power.

Separately, PGE reported a fourth-quarter adjusted profit of $53 million, or 47 cents per share, below analysts’ expectations of 63 cents per share.

Reuters.heading: Exxon committed to moving fast to develop gas in Guyana, executive says
Reuters.summary: Exxon Mobil is committed to rapidly developing natural gas projects in Guyana, according to Dan Ammann, the company’s upstream chief. The move aligns with the Guyanese government's push to increase gas production and supply, including plans for petrochemical plants and data centers. Last year, Exxon introduced the "Wales Gas Vision," an early-stage initiative aiming to supply gas for petrochemical and power projects onshore, but progress has been limited since. Speaking at the Guyana Energy Conference, Ammann noted that natural gas projects are more complex than oil and involve coordination with utilities, communities, and regulators. He stated that a pipeline built by Exxon is ready to deliver gas, pending the completion of power plants scheduled for this year. Ammann emphasized that Exxon will move forward with engineering, execution, and investment as government permits and market frameworks advance.

Reuters.heading: My News
Reuters.summary: Please provide the financial news article you would like me to summarize.

Reuters.heading: Iraq approves 'amicable settlement' with Russia's Lukoil over transfer of operations of giant field
Reuters.summary: On February 17, the Iraqi cabinet approved an "amicable settlement" with Russia's Lukoil regarding the transfer of operations of the West Qurna 2 oil field to the state-run Basra Oil Company. Iraq nationalized West Qurna 2 last month after the U.S. imposed sanctions on Lukoil aimed at pressuring Russia over the war in Ukraine. West Qurna is among the world's largest oilfields, contributing about 0.5% of global oil supply and nearly 10% of Iraq's oil output. Lukoil has until February 28 to sell its assets under U.S. sanctions. Concurrently, Iraq is in discussions with U.S. oil major Chevron to improve contractual terms for West Qurna 2, with any new agreement requiring Iraqi cabinet approval. Chevron’s engagement in the field would expand its presence in Iraq following its recent acquisitions and development agreements. Basra Oil Company has assumed operational control over West Qurna 2 for 12 months pending resolution of ownership matters.

Reuters.heading: Rousey to make MMA return after a decade to fight Carano
Reuters.summary: Former UFC champion Ronda Rousey will return to the cage on May 16 after a decade-long hiatus to fight Gina Carano in a featherweight bout at 145 pounds. The match, announced by Most Valuable Promotions, will be held at the Intuit Dome in Los Angeles and streamed globally on Netflix at no extra cost. The fight will follow the Unified Rules of MMA and consist of five five-minute rounds inside a hexagonal cage. Rousey, 39, holds a 12-2 record with nine submissions and three knockouts, and is a 2008 Olympic judo medallist, former UFC bantamweight champion, and UFC Hall of Fame inductee. Carano, 43, with a 7-1 record, was approached by Rousey for the fight, which Carano confirmed was a long-held desire of Rousey’s. Both women have also acted in Hollywood films, including the "Fast & Furious" franchise.
